speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: And you sat there and picked out the skulls.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, were they just, uh, were the whole thing, was it, was it a bell, was it a bell stock, was it moving all the time?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: It was moving all the time and somebody down below was a hopper tender that emptied them, let them down into the bin below when it got full. And old ladies seemed to, not old ladies, old were girls and young married women all worked at P. Picken. And, um, they sold them for seed, I guess, shipped them over seeds, I don't know, just what they did do with all of them. So each person got his own piece, his own piece, and you wouldn't, uh, nobody else would check yours, you'd have to... No, just the hopper down below, and if you let too many, well probably then next year you wouldn't get called back if you didn't do a pretty good job.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it hard?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: No, not after you got, uh, it was a little bit, um, oh, you get a little dizzy when you first start doing your first year or so, but after you got, you got immune to that and you could just sit there and you didn't dare talk, it was just like school, you know, everything was quiet. You didn't dare go to sleep or anything on the job. I imagine it might have been tempting to go to sleep. Yes, sometimes, for some of them it was, if they'd been up late or something. But there was always a floor boss going around that would talk to you if she thought you were getting sleepy. One woman who's passed away now, she was, um, what did they call her? We just called her boss, but she was a floor walker, Mrs. um, Laylock for 31 years at one place. And so she seen lots of people come and go. 60 women sometimes worked there. At the head of a full crew, it was 60 women.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: All women working on a crew?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yeah, the men worked in the warehouse and they had to keep the bins full in order to keep those, uh, places for us to pick. Yeah, it was all pretty silent, pretty good method. It was a woman's job, I guess. Yeah, never any men picked fees that I know of.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of pay?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Pretty good pay. Of course it wasn't. It started out at probably 25 cents an hour when the pea houses started. And then it got up to about, I don't know what, at the last, what we did get. Probably, um, oh, nothing like they get now. Not two and three dollar, uh, but probably up to a dollar. I can't just remember. It'll be a dollar. Eight hours.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many days? Six, five?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yes, uh, if it was in, uh, a season, why, uh, it would be five days a week. Six, five days a week. Didn't very often work Saturday and Sunday, although sometimes they have a rush order and they'd ask whoever would come back, could come back on Saturday.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do they give you overtime?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, yes. Overtime was always time and a half, you know, them days. And like it is now, I guess, I don't work enough to know, but I'm sure that was time over time. But those days are all gone now. I don't know, well, there's other jobs, I guess. I tell you now, they all get a job up to university, secretaries and file clerks and what have you. That's where all, uh, not the same bunch of peep-pickers, they're almost all dead, but this new bunch of young ones that want to work, that's where they do. And that's what they always said about the, um, farmers when they got too old to do the hard work and milk cows and things. Why, they moved to town and went to work up to university. There's more men working up to university, there's more men, too. Well, men and women both, though. We know several of work, uh, partnership, you know.

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: But you'd say there's a lot more women now working at the university as compared to what there used to be. Sure. Yeah. Yeah. Used to be most of them would have to pick peas or something. Yes, everybody was picking peas, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you do that for very many years?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, I probably only done it about ten years, didn't I? Chilled Social Security until I got old enough for Social Security, then they won't let you work if they know you're that old. But, um, but many, I think, Agnes and Bess and all that bunch, I'll bet they worked twenty-five years at the peas.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Are they from Viola, too?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, no, they're from town, but they're old lodge people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Rebekah?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: No, they're royal neighbors. Royal neighbors, oh. Oh, that didn't mean anything connected with the peas. It just happened that that bunch that I know did work in the peas.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Um, can you just tell me what the techniques were that you had that you used to pick them?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, nothing you can spot, a shriveled up pea or a black pea, or just let the good ones go by. And there wasn't, there were hardly any in there. Some batches it would be just like anything else. Some batches were good and not much to pick out, and some batches would be poor. They'd buy the peas from all the farmers, and some would be good peas, and some would be good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Usually, was there a bunch of different kinds of peas that would get in, or just one or two?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Um, no, I think they were Alaska peas that we used to pick. I think Alaska is the popular pea around, I'm not sure. And then long ago, in the days, you know, when Matty always packed fruit in the fall, you know, until this country went to wheat farmers, there was a big orchard, and there was packing houses all around. And all the young people then packed apples, pears, and what have you. They still do it down to Yakima, I guess, but I think down there they have most machines to sort and everything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, and you?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Just a certain cycle, just like these quilting, you know, that'll all run out after a while. I think it will. I'm not sure what I think it will.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You packed apples in Louisiana, right?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Mm-hmm. As I remember, you told Laura that you, uh, highly spent the money that you made.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, did I?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Well.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But when you packed down there, was that right at the cannery, or where did they do the packing?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: It wasn't the cannery, it was a packing plant. We put them in the boxes, wrapped them in papers, and put them in the boxes, and they were shipped out, like they do at Yakima now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did you stay when you were down there?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, we boarded, um, some of them would, I can remember where I stayed. I only packed down there probably two falls, and I stayed just at a, with a girl who had worked up here in the prune orchard, you know, big prune orchard up on the hill here. And we used to pick prunes, and then later, then I went down there and worked in the apples. But I suppose some of them worked, uh, stayed at the hotel, or something.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you boarded with this girl who came up here then?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: She was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did she board with you when she was in the lab?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: No, she stayed up where, at the chainies, where I lay. Just got acquainted with her, and just got a job down there, so it was a place her mother would board us. And that's the same way when we worked over here at the orchard out of Moscow. A farm woman boarded us. A farm lady just boarded half a dozen of us girls.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: At the orchard we picked?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Not far, probably down the road a mile.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you picking?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: No, I never picked apples. I always just, uh, packed in the packing house.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they packed just in big boxes? Is that how they did it?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Well, ordinary apple boxes that you see now, little unpaired lugs. We always called the pears was lugs. They was not quite as big as apple boxes. But you see them around in the stores now, the big boxes. I don't know how much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: 40 pounds they weighed?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: I think Carlson has an ad in the paper. $3 for a 40 pound box of apples. That's out, you know, the Carlson's. That's Connie's. That's Connie Shefflin's uncle, you know. That's right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, do you remember what you got for that, for the packing?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yes, I think 10 and 12 cents a box was pretty good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many of those were in the packing?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Some people could. The good packers packed 100. They figured to make $10 a day, but I expect 75 and 80 was us kids' limit. Well, even that sounds pretty good. Yeah, it was pretty good for them days. It was pretty good for them days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Like working in the peas or in the packing, did you have much time for socializing? Was there break time?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: In the pea picking, we had five minutes off every hour. You could go get your cup of coffee or anything. In the five minutes every hour.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that enough?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yeah, enough.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You needed that break to just get away from it?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: In the packing, I don't remember what we'd done. In the packing, when you was on your own, you could leave your stand and go any place you wanted for a little bit, I suppose. I suppose if you stayed away too long, you'd get fired. I can't remember that. That's because it was however much you got. Yeah, that was our own lookout. We tried to pack as many as we could, you know. So I suppose we didn't kill much time. I would think that the pea, that the wark and peas would be pretty hard because I would think it, well not hard to do, but the tedious. Well it was, but you got so that you, the women all that worked, most of them had families, and I can remember here in the mind of what they'd done over the weekends. Saturday and Sunday, they say we'd come back, pick peas to rest up. We rest the rest of the week and then we wash an iron and cook and get ready for the next week. And I'm sure that's what we all done.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean you actually worked harder on the weekends?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: On the weekends, sure. That was sittin', you know. And that was, it was kinda boring, but then it wasn't hard work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that would, say, get the people's backs?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, some people complained here and there, but I don't think it ever hurt them. It, well, might have been hard on their eyes. Some people who didn't have good eyes. Yes, it might have been, but I don't, I didn't know anybody that couldn't pick peas in them days. They all could. Yeah, but then this working on the weekends at home, now that sounds like a seven day work week. Well, it was pretty good, but then that was different. And they say, you know, a vacation is anything different. I'd relax just to do the different kinds of things that you had to do. Of course, some of them didn't do anything over the weekend, but most that I knew had families and somebody at home. So.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that for most of the people that did that picking, that it was really necessary to have work for the family and come?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: No, it's extra. It's extra. I'm sure it was. And quite a few were widows. Maybe their husbands had passed away. But a lot of times a husband would work in there, too. She would work in the packing room and he would be down, empty and sacked the peas or something. I know lots of them had their husbands work, too. I don't think they had to work as much as they do now. They got so they lived better. They didn't need as much. Quite as they do now. You know, live simpler. Now people start out when they get married with more than their mothers ever had or think they have to have in the house more. I think that's a lot of it. So there wasn't. That was just extra money for extra things, I think. You said sending the boys to college. That was a lot of it. I think Agnes put half a dozen boys through the university to compete. I guess her husband worked, too, but I can't remember that. Oh, they were just common, ordinary working people. Didn't have any land or anything. Worked in town and she got started to doing that. Every year and then it got pretty good along after. When I first started, we didn't get any unemployment. That got to be quite a racket then. You could draw almost all summer what you'd made in the winter, you know, because that was the good thing about picking peas. When you were out of a job, it was seasonal work. Then you could go on drawing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I guess about half you made, didn't you?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Something like rules about it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many months were you laid off?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Well, we probably began about time school started and then probably end up six months or seven months. All summer was off. Well, then the unemployment lasted most of the summer. Like these fellas, a lot of these fellas now around do that. Well, it seems pretty good, really. Well, it's pretty good if the government just holds out that some of these old ladies that we know get so stingy. I said, you better get a little money in circulation when the government gets to keeping all of us. So I got to get rid of a little money some way. But what I say don't amount to much. But that's one way of thinking about it. You don't want to put it all in the bank. You better keep a little in circulation. Well, I really do think that these days a lot of families feel that both people have to work. Yes, I'm sure they do.

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Well, it's got to cost them so much to go to college and things and to keep up with the Joneses. Why it's a struggle. Well, you better talk to him. Although I think he wants to tell. Oh, well, I usually have the floor. She talked pretty good when I hit around. I do want to talk. I wanted to talk to you about some about your experience in nursing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did she ever talk to Laura much about that?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: No… when we were here last time, we talked a bit about World War I. But I thought maybe you could tell me what that was like.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Could you have been a nurse for long before you went overseas?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, about three years. I nursed in the hospital where I graduated from. Just taking special cases. And then I worked in the shipyard for about a year. And then there was an emergency mark before I went over.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Which hospital was it that you started in?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: St… vincent's apartment.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That wasn't where you started?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, well, I started out in Coos Bay. I worked a little bit with that. I never worked down there. I just went in training. And liked it very well. So I quit. I had to go to the hospital.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that where you went to Coos Bay for your training?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: I went there… my sister and her husband lived there. And I just went there to stay with her a while. Our first baby was born. And then after she didn't leave me in my life, I thought I had to do something. I went out and got a job. Went in training and stayed there at home, I guess. Found six months from it. And I decided I didn't like it. So I quit and I just did practical nursing for a little while. And then I came up the door.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it that you didn't like about it in Coos Bay?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, I don't know. I just didn't like the little hospital. Got approved. I didn't like it. I didn't like nursing, but I just didn't like the place.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you a trained nurse when you went to Coos Bay? Or did you just get it?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: But you could learn it right in the hospital that night. That's what I went in for. I quit and got married. I wasn't a little nursing anymore. And then I went and was talking about war and everything. And I decided I'd go back in and finish it. I heard that nurses worked awfully hard. We did. We worked about 12 hours a day and every day. Sunday and every day. Oh, my. Nothing like they do now. Well, that's an 84-hour work week. That's about twice as long as you would work now. Of course, after I went to St. Vincent's and I finished my training, my family just worked. And we got a case of a day down in Bay.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you mean when you got a case? When they would call you in?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yeah. Not most of the time. Sometimes we'd get a few days off and we'd go off the beach to get away from it, so we wouldn't get called again.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why were they doing it that way? Special cases like that? Were you working kind of temporary or was it?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Just as long as I needed it. The doctor would call for a special nurse and we'd go on. Yeah, but you worked all the time. You was in the hospital when you wasn't out on a special case. Your time went on in the hospital. I got out. After we got off, I was away from the hospital. Sometimes I got called to the only case in the hospital. The nurse lives in Holmes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was a nurse's responsibilities then in those days? What were you expected to?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Whatever was needed. Whatever medication they had to have and whatever they had to do for them. Every case had been different. There was no special things we had to do. We just had to do what was necessary to do for a sick person. I think maybe people didn't always go to the hospital. Now you can hardly get a nurse. If anybody was going to have a baby, they would go to the hospital. They'd call the doctor and the nurse to the home. It was a lot different than nowadays. That's why she was out, I suppose, on the special cases. Once I went with a doctor who was called to go. This was in Coos Bay after I quit nursing, after I quit the training, but was still nursing. We decided to go on a boat. From one of the sluices, she was going to have a baby. We got off the boat and it was about 11 o 'clock at night. I was raining hard and I got off the boat. It was just a little plank. I had to go up and look back and hear the doctor was in the water, and I looked her up to his neck. He had walked right off the boat into the water. He didn't get on the plank at all. We had quite an excitement that night, getting him some dry clothes to wear. He was worse off than the woman who was having a baby. He was more excited than he had. The baby was born when we got there. We had to dry him out and get him warmed up.

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: The baby was already born.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who had delivered the baby?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: I wonder. Oh, he did. After he got out, he was all right with the guy with some clean clothes. The guy with some dry clothes. He shivered around by the fireplace for a little while, and then he was pretty good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that you had a lot more responsibility than a nurse would maybe have today?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: No, I don't know of any more responsibility, but I think it's harder to work more, longer hours. We did lots of things that the nurses don't do now, like making the beds and things like that. They have age to do, and we did all of that then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you very close to the other nurses? Was it kind of like a group of the people who worked together? Did they associate a lot?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, yes, that's all we did associate with all of them training. Didn't hardly meet anybody else. Cascade Hospital. After I got out, I lived with four nurses in the department, and we just take cases from there. I heard the pay wasn't too good for nursing, though. No, not nurses, because they get about as much for eight dollars as we did for the whole fine. Oh, I think more for your eight hours. I think twelve dollars a day was about as much as we ever did get.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you find the work exhausting? Or did you have the energy to go out and do something else when you're nursing?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yes, we nearly always went to the beach when we'd have a day off, or two days off, without the beach and swim and have a good time. Seaside was a great place for swimming pool.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that the doctors had a lot of respect for the nurses then?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, I think it's about the same as today now. I don't think there's much difference. It didn't make any difference.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did it make a lot of difference to the family income that you were working?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, yes, of course.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Never?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Like it is now, it takes two to make a living sometimes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was your husband doing?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: I really didn't live with my husband for about seven years. He was separated. Probably out thirty-three. Never divorced, but we were just separated. So while I was in the Army, I was separated from him. I guess, as I've heard about it, in those days there was a lot more opposition to divorce than there is today. Yes, there was. Someplace they wouldn't take them in training, so they would marry me. I didn't have any trouble getting in the Army.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Had the war started when you got in the Army?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yes, I wasn't in till seventeen, the last part of the war.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you do that, to sign up?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Well, they just asked us all to come up to the hospital and ask us all to sign up. All that wanted to. From my bunch, by the ones that graduated with me, it meant I was thirty-five.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what made you decide you wanted to?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you feel patriotic, or what was the fear?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Just the excitement, I guess, and wanting to go. After I signed up and I got work in the emergency hospital in the shipyard where they were making ships, where I was there for a year, anybody would get a finger hurt or needed medicine or something. I was the only one there, just a midget to nurse each hospital, each shipyard had to have.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there serious injuries that you saw in the shipyard? Serious accidents? Oh, yes, if they were terribly serious, why would we just put them on the ambulance and send them into town?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Most of them just sprained or something like that, which we took care of.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there a doctor there, too?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Just you?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Well, that sounds like a lot of responsibility to me. Well, it was a lot of responsibility.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they have an infirmary or just an office?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Just an office… if they were bad enough to need a leg taken off or something like that, well, we sent them to the front. We didn't do anything. Just put an attorney get on or whatever was necessary and send them in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why not?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: I don't think so.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he living on the bus? Boy, there was a lot of activity there in those shipyards, wasn't there?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yes. Yes, I know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He's fifteen now, wasn't he?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Well, sure. Sure.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how is it decided that you were going to go overseas? Did they just pick you to go?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: The hospital formed, Base Hospital, 46, formed there. They were all Portland doctors and they just called the nurses. No. Formed a hospital unit. There was a hundred nurses and I don't remember, about seventy-five doctors. And a lot of core boys. Well, that formed a hospital. I don't know that. That don't sound like the first.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And what, where?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: We went right over there. Oh, well, there was, see, about ten of us went to North Carolina first. Oh, I see. There was a hospital there. He had time to think and write about it. Well, he wouldn't, if it wasn't that way, he never went to North Carolina. I was there for quite a while and he was sitting there, whenever there was a boat.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when I hear Mary think I'm a shooter, anybody else?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: I'll hold on to the army. I think so. Jack and him were.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what kind of setup did you have in Europe? What kind of a hospital was it that put you in?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: It was a big barracks. Oh, sure. We went to a little place called Beaulieu, down in the southern part of France. And that was, I think, three different hospitals there, two big hospitals. Oh, yeah. And we all went there. And they had barracks. He's very, he's had about ten or twelve patients and there was an award there, just like them. Maybe. Maybe in a hospital. Maybe. Took care of the award. No. And then I was out in the field hospital, just an operating team.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it right behind the lions and just did operating?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: I expected that. Send them right back. They didn't have any beds or anything like that. No. Well, I'm out in operating room, table and operated and then sent them back to the hospital. Yeah. That was called Field Hospital. Oh, yeah. Send them back to the base hospital.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So was it?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: So the emergency hospital was the one with the barracks. Mm-hmm.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when you were at the field hospital, you stayed there and slept there and everything, right? What kind of quarters was that? Just ten?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Whatever they could give us. Sometimes it was in an old hospital or a church or something. Sometimes it was right out in the open. I was out for ten. Think about him, just worry. Sometimes they... No, nothing. Well, that was always operating in tents. Just put up the tent. So would you... Fix an operating room.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How big was that staff at the Field Hospital? Big was what? The staff, how many doctors and nurses?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Two doctors and nurses to each operating team. Well... And some boys... Oh, yeah. Fix up the tents and do things like that. Fix up the... Pants, you know, get wet there and... Mm-hmm. Take care of all the stretchers and all that. I think so. Big dog. The men that came in there must have been pretty bad shape. Sure. They didn't believe in who it was, but if they were able to go back on it, they just died back in the base hospital. Base hospital, which was just the ones already. I know it. Took out shells and things like that. Emergency work. Sure. But, you know, in the years that he's been gone, maybe... He's been moving around, sometimes his eye, sometimes his legs, sometimes...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What the kind injuries that men were getting there, was it mostly just from bullets, or was it from worse than that?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Whatever it was, from mostly bullets, yeah. Well, that's a trap. Take it out of shock. That. Sometimes they were bleeding, they'd put a tourniquet on their arm or leg, or take out shrapnel from their... You just can't imagine. Sometimes their face was just full of shrapnel, and they'd been shot, and you'd have to take that out. Everything that had to be done right away. Something out of their eyes, maybe stealing their eye. And then we'd just put them on the stretchers and send them back to the hospital.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you have to operate with under those circumstances?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Did you have any good... Carry their kits, just the same as they do in the hospital. They had their instruments and everything. They had to sterilize them, keep them clean. And we had... We'd get just big packages of sterile gauze and things like that already prepared.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there much anesthetic available then?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yeah, we had them set a mast with us too. Nothing else could give either. Besides the two doctors. That sounds like it must have been really rough. Oh, it was hard and long. Sometimes it was up all night.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Kind of interesting though, don't you think?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, yes. Fascinating… and then when I went back to the base, in the last two months I was in a flu ward. I suppose it was what they call flying flu now. No. We just called it flu. And the worst kinds were flu in the morning. And I was in that ward when there was just a sign.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that the same flu that hit here?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yes, at that time. But I think it's a little different than flying flu now. I didn't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that really killing a lot of people over there?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yes, sometimes four and five in one night. She thought the boys would all be dead before they got a chance to get home and the war was over. They was all sick.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was that bad?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yes, that was awful bad. It was bad everywhere. Both of these were worse over there because we didn't have the facilities to stay care of. Too many people in one place, they always said. You know, groups of people you weren't supposed to.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How would it say, did that get half of the, would you say half of the boys came down with it at that time?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, I don't know whether it be half or not, but there was an awful lot of them. Every convoy that come in to that, you just flew patients. And some of them would just have flu and get them to work and go back. And some of them would get in the middle of the mountain and die.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the treatment that they had you give them?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Well, that was the trouble. They didn't have much treatment. They just, we'd give a hypodermic of something quiet and then something to do for the pain. Sometimes four and five a night. It'd take every four hours. We'd have to do hypodermic. But there just wasn't much too, too far. Thank you. Homemade. Gosh, it kind of went bad. Good. Well, that tastes pretty good, but it's, it didn't do creamy like it should.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, in the field hospital, when you were in the field hospital, can you tell me kind of how you felt, doing that work and seeing what you saw? And what do you mean?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Well, I was just thinking if it didn't make you... Did you have an emotional... Yeah, emotionally.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did it have to make you feel?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Well, yes, of course. I was about young, but in the man now. I didn't worry as much I guess, but it's no pleasure to see people die. Someone begging you to do something for them to let them go home and all that… it was bad… i know. I think if for me it would have been, I mean, I don't think I could have done it. I'd rather be fighting and seeing it. But you learn all that when you're training to be a nurse. I think you get immune to a lot of things. Oh, the blood and all that didn't faze me at all. I could work any kind of operation. Or you could get that way when it's your work. And after I come home, I couldn't nurse anymore. I don't know anything. I don't know. I wasn't nervous or I wasn't shaky or anything like that, but I just all worked up inside. I didn't like to take a case. Well, I just didn't like to nurse. I don't know. I did at some, but it was different.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that's because of what you'd experienced over there?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Because of it. Because of what you've seen. And then I got this back trouble, and that didn't help any.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you tell him how you got your back?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Well... Oh, I knew we were just packing up to come home to start. And I don't know just how I got it. I just sprained it some. You always told me, you and a nurse picked up a...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Trunk or something?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Trunk. And she slipped, and that done her back up. I thought that was what it did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And it didn't heal up, right? It didn't just get better?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: It didn't get better. It didn't just get better. Oh, no. I was just down. I couldn't move for a while, and they took care of me there and wanted me to stay on there across at the other hospital, but I thought I wanted to come home, so they put me on a stretcher, and I come about halfway on a stretcher. And then she suffered with it ever since.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Really?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: She's a disabled war veteran. She gets a pretty good pension out of that, because she is a disabled. And she used to have to go to the hospital every so often to see how bad it was, and then that determined what her next check would be, if it was worse or if it was better. Never got better. I got a little bit more each time I went. And now it's settled down to one. When she gets old, why then she don't go anymore to have it. It's just an injury, and that's all. It won't get any better or worse, probably.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you really couldn't work regularly after that?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: I couldn't. There's a lot of things I couldn't do. I couldn't give hypodermics like I used to. I had shots, you know. And it just shake me up so I just don't know. I couldn't do it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was from your back or not?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: I don't know. Oh. My back was just nervousness from giving some in over there. I used to give sometimes 40 in a night. So it was 12 or 14 patients, and they had to take a hypodermic every four hours. That's why it just kept me about busy. Stylizing my needle and going through the next one.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I suppose that would be an emotional strain, wouldn't it?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yeah. And sometimes in the middle of the night, we had to have a flashlight. That's all the light we could have because of that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you and the doctors have to work sometimes at 24 hours in a row without stopping and that kind of thing?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Well, not 24 hours. If we worked all night, that's why we got off at 9 or 10 o 'clock. See, there was two teams at the same place. And then they'd come on and let us rest, and then we'd go on and let them rest. So we didn't work awful many hours. We worked a long time sometimes. I remember standing up and grass halfway up to my knees all night, operating, mud.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And doing it right outside?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, well, there's no floors to the tents, you know. Just go in. If it was raining hard, some of them would leak. We'd put up with a lot of things. One time, we fixed the emergency hospital in an old French hospital where the sisters had been, and they moved them out. Then we'd come in, and all the house was dirty. We cleaned it up just a couple of rooms and used them for our things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how much sterility, sterilizing could you do under those conditions?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Just boil… they had little burners, and we'd boil our instruments. But like in the mud and the grass, I mean, it doesn't sound like... No, we just have to fix a table and put a sterilized cloth on and put our instruments on that. The rest of it was just like any tent.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were most of the injured men young?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, yes… here they all did, you know, except the officers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And they didn't get hurt very bad, did they?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Maybe stay now. Yes, it was nearly all just young fellas.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Could you tell how most of them took it? Were many of them scared, frightened?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Some of them, not very many. They didn't show it anyway. They were hurt. They were just hurt like any other boy. They waited to be fixed up. Tell him about when you went over. How some of them boys had never been away from home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you say? They knocked them in the head or knocked them out? What did they do?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: They said they had to knock some of them out to get them on a boat. To go, you know, they just couldn't do it. I can imagine how it would be. He hadn't been away from home and just scared to death. Dan, I've heard that before. Well, the little that I've heard about the fighting and the conditions of the fighting during World War I just sounds awful. I mean, it sounds like they were all in foxholes and they had... Well, of course they were. That's how most of the fighting was. One night we had about 40 boys come in. They was in a forest somewhere and stopped for the night and a shell came down and hit them. They were just full of shell shrapnel and everything. They brought them all in there to be. Some of them were there, arms full of shrapnel and some of them were broken arms and just everything. I expect it was kind of unpredictable. You didn't know just what was going to happen. Just everything. One night, or day or night, we took care of the Germans too sometimes, you know. We got a prisoner while we took care of him. There was one little prisoner, all that, I was 40, more than 18 years old. He was so scared. One of our doctors talked German. He was talking German to him and he was so scared. He didn't know what he was saying.

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: He said, what's the matter with you, boy? Don't you know I'm speaking your language. He was so scared. He didn't know what he was doing to be and what was his enemies too besides being hurt. After I backed him to the barracks, there was a old German, a old believe it was a German boy. The boys wouldn't take care of him because he was an enemy. They wanted to kill him. We tried to do all the time and so we had to keep them out and we had to take care of them. Oh dear. The fourth thing I felt was sorry for him. He was no different than anybody else, but he just happened to be on the wrong side of the fence. We kept him there for a long time and he was well enough to leave.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I think this is probably a hard question, but I'm wondering, do you recollect how the boys faced death and dying as young people?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: How they faced it. I imagine you saw a lot of those boys die. Well, I never was around the gas ones very much, but I know there were an awful lot of them that got gassed. No, but he means just when they're going to die. Them died with a flu.

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: How did they react?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Well, most of them were unconscious quite a while before they died. And some didn't want them dying like everybody else.

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Do you think we'll have more wars here?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: I don't doubt it. Since World War I was the war to end all wars, we seem to have had quite a few. But you know, well, I'm just connected with that. When my youngest boy started to school, there was about eight little boys all had been born at the time. They were six years old when they started to school. I think there was just one girl in the bunch. And one old lady, a neighbor up the creek, she said, you know, when there's so many boy babies born, that means war. And sure enough, I bet every one of them, there's one now, my kid is here, and every one of that bunch of boys that started to school here at Viola that were six years old at a certain time, they went in that World War II.

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Just every one of them, two gets boys, and I can name every one of them, I do believe, maybe one was a 4F, but wasn't that funny?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: She predicted that. She was an old lady and she said that. That means war. Well, I don't think that it didn't need to do with it. Just happened to hit that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you believe it at the time that that was going to be the last war for us?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yes, I suppose I did. I guess everybody thought it, but the Bible said there's always wars and rumors of war. Well, I don't doubt that, but I don't see why they all have to involve us. I know, I don't either. And I don't see what good it does fighting.

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Why don't they quit making that stuff?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: They couldn't shoot themselves if they didn't have guns. It makes me so mad that this, the United States has to furnish them all with the material to shoot back at them. I think I could be smarter than that, and they're supposed to be smart men. They can see that if they don't have the ammunition, you can shoot. Well, they feel that if it didn't have one country would dominate. Yes, but if they don't, just quit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's the use of killing each other off? Don't you think it has something to do with the fact that there's money to be made in selling all that stuff?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: There's money to be made, and too many people. You've got to send out the people. That's too much money. What it is, too many people. Yes. Well, that sounds to me like being a nurse under the roughest conditions that I can imagine. Oh, I think World War I was rough.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you feel great satisfaction out of that work that you were doing there?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yes, we knew it was necessary. We knew it had to be done. Did the doctors, when they decided who they're going to operate on and how, and all that, did they just let a lot of people go who they thought wouldn't make it? Oh, no, I don't think so. I think they operated whenever it was necessary. Sometimes when it wasn't the same as they do now, I think they do an awful lot of operations now that aren't necessary. What would make them decide, say, if they had to take an arm off, what would be the... Well, of course, they'd have to decide whether it could be, if there was any chance of healing itself or being healed, I'd send them back to another hospital or back home. They had to do their own deciding, just like they do now. Doctor will tell you what it has to come on, if it's gang-in-this or infected or anything like that, they know it has to come on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They didn't have much time to make a decision about that, did they?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: They didn't have to think about it, they just had to make a quick decision, but probably made a lot of mistakes. It seems to me that it must have taken a lot of bravery on her part to be in... I think you're under kind of a nervous strain. You just... The shells were going over our heads, and they kind of told us to get back up and go out. Well, we knew we couldn't just pack up and leave all the patients there, so we just stayed. After we got our citation for staying, it was dis... Contempt a cart or something to that effect.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, you mean you got a bad citation for bravery after it was over?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Well, at the time it was a disobeying order, because they told us to move back so many miles behind the mines we're supposed to. We didn't any of us do it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you feel... the people you were working with, the doctors and the nurses, did you become very close to each other through doing that kind of work?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, yes, I guess so. I think it's the same as it is now. In the hospitals they say a man gets sick and has a good nurse, he always proposes before he goes home, I think the same way then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You get a lot of proposals?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Well, fortunately, he says people are real rich. And if I could just get him, I don't get him home. He said I could just have all kinds of money. I think it's the same today as always. You know, it's just the same as the others. I think so. Well, I would think that being over there with the boys would not only be homesick, but they wouldn't have a chance to see women at all either. I think. They were pretty lonely, I'm sure. And any woman looked good to them, I think. That was about the way it was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How old were you when you were over there?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: About thirty… so I wasn't awfully young, but still I wasn't. But there would have been lots younger. Twenty-five.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And quite a few younger?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yes, I think them old ones went too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: As far as the conditions of living for you personally, for the nurses over there, could you keep clean to your satisfaction?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yes, we managed to do pretty good. It was just kind of, sometimes kind of a crude way, but then we managed to get our washing done most of the time and things like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your own?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yeah, lots of times. But they had pretty good facilities too, considering everything. They had a laundry, and to do sheets and things like that for the boys, you know. And I would send our uniforms to. It just sometimes got misplaced, I think is all. Well, I was out in the field hospital while we didn't. I remember that long cape with a red lining, blue cape with a red lining that the nurses all wore, and we was all kind of proud of them when I had it when I was in the hospital down in North Carolina. And they shipped them over, you know. And so I was supposed to get it when I got over there, but we never did get them to the girls that were in the field hospital. Never did get them to get back to base, and I was kind of mad because I didn't get my cape. And so many things we didn't get. We didn't have change of clothes and all that. I was sent back to every time, Mr. Guenther.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you really only had the uniform that you were wearing, and that was it?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: No, I think we had a few extras, but we just packed enough up from the went. But it's like it is now. They get on the wrong plane or wrong something, and had a little worse in wartime.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have any time off over there?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yes. The night that we got word that the armistice was going to be signed about three days before the rest of y 'all got it here for the real armistice. And the night the armistice was signed, I took two or three other nurses and I put in our nails. Well, we all put in our nails for, they said we could go on vacation, take turns, you know. So I was one of the first ones I would bounce that got to go with three other nurses. And we went to Niece and Monte Carlo and down on the river area for a week, and we had a good time. One day we went up in the, what they call the Maritime Alps, the big mountains there, and watched them make beer, make wine from the grapes. And we ate way up in the little restaurant they had up there. They just had a, it's kind of a, like it is in San Francisco where they brought those hills of the carton and the trolleys. The trolleys. The trolley cartons. We took the day off and went up there. There was two southern lieutenants that were going the same way, so we matched up with them. We hadn't been there and we had it off all the time that day. And then another day we went to the casino at the Monte Carlo where they played cards one evening. I had a dollar to show for her, I guess I have it, yeah, someplace.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, you made money instead of losing it?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: No, I didn't play. They wouldn't let us play. In fact, they wouldn't let us in. Girls had to be escorted in. So there happened to be a captain standing there when we were trying to get in. He said, I'll take you in. So we grabbed two of us, Jean, the fad, and then I, and we went in and we went through and watched them play in and saw all that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They didn't allow women to play? Could they play in there?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, yes, they were playing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why couldn't you women get in?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Had to have an escort maybe.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Couldn't anybody play with uniforms?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Either men or women. Oh, I guess that was. They wouldn't let the girls in unless they were escorted by some man. So we got in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that the first time you'd had off since you were over?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: That was the first time. That was the first and only time we had off.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long were you there?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, you'd have rest periods, wouldn't you?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Well, I was just in the village. Yes, but that was... I never was away from that. No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there any social life for you in the camp when you were working?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, yes. I remember we made a Christmas tree and fixed up for the orphans home there.

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Well, I think you had dances and everything around base, didn't you?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: No, not around base. We did... I think they got to go in the camp. Some of the... I don't know where they took care of them in San Ferrer.

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yeah, what did they call that?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Where they served coffee and you went, where the boys all went.

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yeah, what do you call them? Esso, what do they call them? You see them, Andrew Dyer?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yeah, yeah. It's the... You'd have to get an invitation to call them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, now what?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: UFO camps. Yeah, that's right. Yes, I think they have them every place. Maybe a little far away some of them, but... Well, now, UFO, that's flying saucers. I never got them. No, I don't know. I know what you're trying to think of. You know what we need. Sure, sure. But I think we got the wrong initials on it. The U... I don't know what they didn't call them. Well, I don't know that they had them every place you could see them advertised yet. Well, as far as the work goes of being a nurse and the long days and all the hours that you put in, I mean, to me, I think about it today, and it's hard to imagine people working that long, that hard.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did it seem to you at the time to be demanding? Demanding a lot of you?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Everybody would have had long hours those days. You know, I was in training, you know, we didn't have a whole day off. We had a half day once a week. Well, I don't think it was any more confining than it is to be a nurse in the hospital right now. You've got, I think, they... But the 12-hour days, seven days a week that she talks about, that seems... We never heard of an eight-hour day. Ever heard of a...

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: What about a coffee break?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Grown up in a half-a-day coffee break… wait a minute.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You say you get up at four and you go to bed at nine?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Well, probably. You're as lucky if you go out to bed at nine when you're cooking for 24 men out in the field and cook houses.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How old were you when you started doing that?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Well, I... About 16. And then, a few times later, I had a helper and I went ahead. So I done that. I cooked him a good cost for quite a few years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How hard would that work?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: That was something. That was real work. You had a flunky who had a rig with a horse and he got the water and brought the groceries in. About the time you think you had everything done, they'd come and tell you to move. We'd go on a move. They was done with that field. Get everything up and move. Get your dishes packed up and move. Maybe have bread baking in the oven. Baked all our own bread them days for the men. That was real work, but it didn't last very long. No. You're as lucky if you got in 30 days or 25, 30, or maybe 35.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you didn't know in advance from when you would be moving?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, the flunky would tell us. Probably we'll move this afternoon. But maybe on short orders, he didn't know either. He'd be down to get meat and stuff. And when he'd come back, they'd run it through a little faster than he thought. And that's the way it was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How set down could you get your routine? Was it a struggle to get it all done on top of it?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, yes. But then, oh, they was all good. But just so long as you had something to eat, but you had to figure. You always had pie for dinner and cake or cookies for supper, and we'd send out lunch. Some of them had lunch in the morning and some of them lunch in the afternoon. Some of them, I guess, had lunches too. I don't think we ever had lunch only once a day and make sandwiches and coffee.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you served only three meals?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Three meals and one lunch. So that's quite a bit of work too. Three meals and one lunch. One lunch, mm-hmm. But now, you know you have somebody come to fix your house or do something. They bring their lunch. You're not supposed to cook for anybody.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Ain't that funny?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: And you used to be cooked for everybody. Yes, even a traveling man is going through, and our mother's seeing her come up. She'd start getting meal ready.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Really?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Well, we had, you know, we had McNess man and Rawleys, them traveling, men that peddled your pepper and cinnamon and vanilla. Well, that was quite an event when they come to your house. They were right. So your partner had to feed them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And lots of times, if it was night, you had barn room, why they stayed all night?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Of course, they paid for it, but they liked to visit too, and they were anxious to get acquainted with people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You said Rawley, and what was the other one?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Rawley and McNess. McNess.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would they stay, would they sleep in the house?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: They're the same. Oh, usually, but sometimes they'd have their bed along and go to the barn and sleep, but I think them, sometimes it was neighbors doing that, why they'd sleep in the house. Yeah, times have changed. You see that on television now, and them traveling, men going.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: One old program, what was that?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Where he peddled all the lace and stuff. It was an event, though. Yes, them days it was. When there wasn't, you had to go to town with the team, you know, and so it was quite an event when they come. They were going in all days to get a little girl. Right. Didn't go on twenty minutes like we do now. I'll bet that the food that you served in Thrashing Creek must have been pretty satisfactory. Oh, it was. Everybody had big gardens, spuds and potatoes and vegetables, and the meat men, if they didn't go to town to buy it, the meat men would butcher and travel around to get rid of their beef, you know, to the different, oh well, though, in this section of the country, it'd probably be a half a dozen thrashing machines, you know, and it was pretty good money to peddle your meat around.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you serve, did everybody come inside the cookhouse to eat? How was it set up? What was it like on the inside?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Well, probably not quite as wide as this, and some of them had long tables on either side, and some of them had little square tables that let four sit around. We've had both kinds. I've been in both kinds.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many men could you sit in there?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Well, yeah, usually the whole crew. Maybe the roustabout or some of the bosses or somebody, oilers out the machine wouldn't come in right when, but the bundle haulers and all that was usually, you're supposed to serve them all the same time. I don't know. You just got in and done. I couldn't do it now. I couldn't think of. I don't know. Well, I would think that cooking for 20, 25 people after just cooking for your family would be quite a change. Well, it was, but then you adjusted to it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you learn from the, from what, you started helping somebody else? Did you learn from them?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, I suppose I learned. But you kind of grew up with it. You knew about cook houses and took all the depth to get to work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it pretty good?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yes… that's a good way. Yes, five dollars a day for them days was really good. That sounds good. Now, well, it won't happen anymore.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You've probably seen an old cook house, have you?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: I've never seen a cook house, but I've seen lots of pictures. Yes. Yes, they had screen windows and with a flap down probably when it was cold for mornings. Plus you got pretty hot in there too. Get pretty hot and pretty many flies, you know, had to have those sticky things to keep the flies. But of course we moved. I guess when you moved and didn't set too long in one place, the flies couldn't catch up with you too good. But when it'd be a rainy spell, but of course most of the men would be neighbors. They'd go home in a rainy spell. You wouldn't have so many. So then you could kind of relax and rest. Well, I think I ever had those over on friends one time. We were moving. I don't know how it happened. They'd go in, but we stopped at a little place where they said we could eat. And we stopped and sat down at the table and the yellow jackets were so sweet, we couldn't hardly see the flies. Oh, that was terrible. That was in France. That was in France. Yeah. Sometimes we didn't have very much to eat for a day or two. Sometimes we wouldn't get up in time. If you eat it late or something, we'd be pretty hungry. We told us the place to eat with you. We're pretty happy about it. But I can remember when Wade was in the Army, he said he felt bad sometimes. They'd be packed up to move and sit and wait. And he said it's a waiting game always, you know. And get orders to move. And he said all that good food would be just left. He said it was such a waste.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Which war was it here? Two?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yes, two. But he was one of those that were too old. They didn't keep him very long.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But he was in Australia, you remember? Where they was working?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: I don't know if they were digging ditches or what. But he said he got so mad he was around probably 30-some. They sent some of them from here, every place. And then when they got enough younger boys, they let those guys out. But he was there quite a while. They said them young smart Alex would come and tell him how to groom his horses. He said, I've carried a horse all my life from the farm, you know. And he didn't like it a bit. Tell him how to take care of his horses. I remember one time we went back to Paris with pet orders. We did that between down here. When we got to this one fight, we went back and maybe stayed in Paris for a day or two. So one of the doctors was going to give us a real dinner, he said. And we gathered an oral order and just down and they would sit down and orders for us to go. We had to get packed up and made a few sandwiches. Took what we could and we didn't get to eat it.

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh boy. Well I imagine in a field hospital you probably weren't in a very good place to get good eats either. I think they had lots. It was kind of a bit sometimes it didn't get cooked very good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well you know in the thrashing crews, did you find that an exciting time for all of us?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yes. Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What made it that way?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh you was young and moving around. I don't know what made it exciting.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Everything was exciting wasn't it when you was young?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Well I don't know. You get pretty tired. I wondered if it wasn't somewhat the fact that all the community was kind of getting together on it. Well a lot of times you knew most of the men who were working. And you felt kind of good to see them eat. And oh I don't know it was just hard work and I suppose wanted the money mostly. I know a number of people that met their wives, their husbands. I expect that's probably one of the things that was attractive too. The boys were usually doing pretty nice to the girls that were cooking. I think that's something about it too. Offering to do the dishes and that sort of thing. Help a little. Well I don't know. I never met anybody helping do dishes because they were usually working most of the time that we were. And pretty tired when night come. But probably a little of that on rainy days kind of hanging around probably.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have a chance to visit during that time? Visit with the women kind of saying on the farm?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh not much. We was usually sitting out in the field. But sometimes we'd have company. Sometimes other kids would come on horseback. Some of the girlfriends. Everybody rode horses them days you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you have breaks during the day when you had everything square away?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Well sometimes but if you had a move or two you was pretty busy most all the time. Well this move came.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you stay at the cook house when it moved?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yeah sometimes and sometimes we'd ride with a rust about. But I guess we had to ride in the cook house most of the time to see that the water barrel didn't tip over and a few things like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well what about everything else?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Well we had places to put them. The benches if we had the long table where they sat was benches and you piled everything in the benches. I don't remember that. Right inside the benches. Lid on and we just set them in the benches.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there enough room in the cook house?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh yes I didn't see much. You didn't feel it was a... No we had a good table and a work... I mean a good stove and a work table. Well you had all the room you needed I guess. I think so. Of course you didn't know any different. You had to do it and you just done it. I think that was the way of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you work to go to school?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: It was in Viola that you came in at State. It was in Moscow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was Moscow to high school?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Although my mother when I was the first grader she lived up here at the hotel. We had a three story hotel here in Viola and we lived three miles up the creek.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you stay on the way?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Friday night. That's how bad the roads was to get three miles you know. Mother would stay at the hotel and the kids would stay with her.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many of you kids?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Well usually three of us wasn't there. I don't think Maddy ever stayed with us.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Going to school?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And this would be for the whole winter?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yeah all winter till spring and probably she'd move home when work started. But during the bad weather we had a room at the... two rooms I can remember.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you do this for a number of years? Two or three years didn't we?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: I think so… to the bad weather. We used to get deep snow. I like it now. I don't know why. So then your father would be batching it. He was batching and as a rule that's how they made their living. My dad would make wood all winter and sell it in the summer before he got enough... land broke to raise hay and things. And he would make wood and he usually had a couple of friends there that worked for him. And they batched and made wood. I remember them fellas from way down at Guy, they used to come and stay up there and make wood. Yeah that was what people... how people made their living. They sold wood to the farmer. That was right here. Right around here. Three miles east of here is where I was born. Where is the line between the timber and the...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: prairie here? Isn't this pretty close to here?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Just about right along here. Up above of course it's broke out. More land is broke out now. But all up our road from Viola East there was trees. There's been lots of land broke out and on our place up there that was all timber. And there's been sawmills. Well there was a sawmill. I can remember the sawdust when we... I never saw the mill but before we lived there there was a sawmill. And then the Manson's had the sawmill and several different hollows up the creek. They logged all the mountain off up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he homestead the place?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: My dad didn't homestead. He bought a preemption right. Somebody had homesteaded it and let it go back. And that's where my dad got that. But he said when they came out here... It's funny this gal lives next door. Her dad and my dad come on the train.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of a train did they call them?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: It wasn't. It was... Steam engine. I know but they called them when they brought people west. There's a name for it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You would bring a car with you?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: I think so. Take a lot of people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And their belongings too, right?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yeah. Well they come and worked a year. My dad did and I suppose Jim Rothfeld did too. They worked a year to see how they was going to like it at the mill someplace away up here. And then he went back and brought the folks out. But she said she'd come alone with five kids.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: My mother didn't she?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: I was born after they came out here. I think he came back but he had to come back to work and then she came back. Yeah. She sold off what they had and come. She always said it wasn't such a good place. She said they'd probably left if they ever had money to leave. But they couldn't get money to go anyplace else. But she said that. But I've heard her say this was nearest heaven too because there wasn't the bugs and the storms. That's why they left back there, the hail storms. They come Iowa and Kansas. They lived in both places. But that was awful hard winters in Iowa. And Kansas, I guess that was a storm or vice versa. Yeah, the storms would be a storm. Yeah, hail. Terrible storms. They had their homes to back there. No, but they owned some land. My father's folks were farmed in Iowa.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I believe it was live, wasn't it?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yeah. But it wasn't bad. They had a pretty good life. Well, what I started to tell my dad said all the way from Walla Walla was railroad land that they could have filed on. Big strips along that they could have had. He could have had a good farm from here to Walla Walla. But instead of that, he come to the mountains because he said he'd been on the prairie and knew about the storms and was afraid. He always wondered if he made a mistake. He said he reminded him of Scotland. In the woods. But he made a good enough living. We didn't have much. But everybody else worked hard for it, but raised everything we ate. We never was so hard up. My dad had cattle then when he got a little lamb broke, made wood. I guess we never had much money, but I guess we lived about as good as other people, I think, probably. And later we had picked strawberries. Yes, and that got to be strawberry country up in the hills here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Started growing them?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yes, and we raised strawberries.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there a market?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, yes… remember, $4 of cray we thought was an awful price. Got to be a lot more than that. Got to be $4. A half a crate. A flat.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much did he put a fair bit of his land into it?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yes, we had a whole hillside, and you'd rotate them, you know. And you had to keep putting in. But there was Tour 3 that had big patches up in our hills. But now nobody has even enough for her. But my daughter here, we picked strawberries the other day. We've had strawberries since decoration day. She's got ever bearings, and I've never seen the like. But she sets out a new row or two, and that's where you get your berries for the next year.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did they start canning?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Canning. I don't know. I can remember drying everything when my mother was drying corn.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that the way you preserved it still when we went?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yes, canned too, I mean. Yes, but...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So they were canned and dried?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: I think so.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So they were canning when you were 10?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, yes. When I was young, yeah, they canned.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But did they do it in jars?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yes, I think...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they use paraffin then, or what did they use to see?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: I don't... I think we had fruit jars just the same all during my lifetime. And then there was a little time a few years ago when they got... everybody got freezers, and they done a lot of it in that, but now they're getting a can again. I'm sure. Of course, everybody has a freezer yet for meat and things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
