words: You went into law school and go to high school, is that right? Did you work when you went in there? You didn't work for your board or anything?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Well, I did one year too, and that all you girls had... Two or three years. Yeah, you did. All my sisters had worked for their board. You see, I was... she went to high school about my second year, so it was quite a stretch. I was still out here going to school. But then, when I graduated in the eighth grade, then I went in too. She was going to university then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did you board?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Well, we had rented rooms and batched. Three, four girls of us.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Rented in someones?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Homes, rent... yeah, just like apartments. Now, we didn't have apartments, but people rented out rooms, and put a stove up in the kitchen and a bed, and it wasn't like the apartments now, but they were livable.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And did you work for your board?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: I didn't. I was only nine years old the first time I went to school in Moscow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That young? Nine?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Nine, yeah, because the girls were going to school. They were older. But then, I went to school that year, and then I went to school out here again. I suppose I just wanted to go because the big girls were going to school in there. I suppose that was the reason they took me. Yes, that was the idea. From up home here, I couldn't get down to Viola alone.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, where are the kids going?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, yeah, but nobody from our house. And the other girls was all in town, so they let me...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you lived with them?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Mm-hmm.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you all live together as a girl?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Mm-hmm. Upstairs, two rooms we had.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you always went to school?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Batched. I was always just to work. She was taken dressmaking.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how did that work? Did you girls take care of her and keep sharp eye on her and tell her what to do now?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: I guess. I was in age grade the year we met. I was probably pretty sharp. She'd always interact her bread and all she could carry... She got quarters worth of bread she could hardly carry at home. Yeah, you got six loaves for a quarter, and that used to make me so mad when they'd send me after bread, and I had to carry all that six loaves. Now I'd be happy to get six loaves for a quarter.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I have to listen pretty closely to what your sisters told you to do, or did you have the independence to do pretty much what you wanted?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Thank you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Don't you think? Huh?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: He wants to know if I minded pretty good. I think our older sister was a pretty... Kept us pretty much in line. She was five years older than me, and I had only nine years. Yeah, I think probably I did too. But this sister, we got a sister up in Spokane in a home now. She's older than Jenny, pretty spry. Her mind is pretty alert, and she still tells yet when they used to be playing games or cards at the table at home in the winter, I said, I kicked up such a ruckus, and she said she could still see my dad looking over his paper and saying, let that kid beat once. If they beat me every time, I'd kick up an awful stink, I'd be mad about it. She said, I can still see our dad say, let that kid beat. Let that kid beat in a game. If they beat me, I would be mad about it and be pretty noisy. That's what she said. She could still see him looking over his paper and...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you figure that that was kind of rough to have to be separated as a family to go to school and that kind of thing?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, I don't think we ever thought about that. Something new to get to you to live in town. I don't think so. New... Drove a team. She's a breast-fried chicken. I remember early the day you raised her.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They were some hares, had they?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: She'd pry one of those and bring it to us. Yeah, I think we was always glad to see her come, and I guess she probably was glad to get rid of us for a little while maybe. We used to come home, not every week, but I can remember getting up early at home, and our dad would get us to town at 9 o 'clock with a team, you know, so that meant starting up there pretty early. On Monday morning, sometimes we would come home, you know, and stay the weekend, and he'd get us to school. But there was no school in Viola for high school. Then there wasn't, but my daughter went to high school. Afterwards, we had good school, and I don't know if we ever had four years, but we had two. I think we, no, didn't have only eighth grade when you were, but when Shirley did, I... But your sisters were, you girls were going through high school at the time except for the oldest one and the youngest one.

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: The oldest one, I mean. This, at this one I... Oh, so you were really on your own. I mean, you must have all been pretty independent then, living on your own. Oh, I guess it wasn't hard to get a... cook a little and get up and go to school. I guess that was the main thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they have much social life?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, I'm sure they did. What did you do for your... Yes, I know I had the measles that year, too. Yeah, but he said social life. I was thinking of what you did for your recreation in Moscow. Oh, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you go to dances and that sort of thing?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, yes, we had parties. We were going to have a party tonight, but I graduated in eighth grade and I couldn't go for the measles and I had the girls...some of the other girls brought my diploma to me on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that eighth grade?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: That was eighth grade.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, you had...didn't you have measles when you went to university? Have anyone? Measles?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: No, I was in eighth grade. You did. Well, I can't remember everything. I had to stay in a dark room and I had to stay in bed. That must have been a big disappointment. I expect it was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, is that Moscow or here?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: We must have been there, too, but...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they live there one year?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: I think a couple or three years we lived there, that place.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you go to high school then later yourself in Moscow?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yes, these girls were all gone, some of them married, and another girl and I batched and I went to high school. The two of you lived in Moscow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was that like for you?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, that was we, I thought we was pretty smart young lady. We used to come out here on Friday night and go to party or dance or something. Just like they do now, I guess. We all didn't have cars and things like they do now to run.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have horses in town?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, we didn't have horses in town, although I had a horse to ride when I stayed at canals. So, just pretty much like it is now, I guess.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever go to any of the reliability they had in Moscow?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: At that time there were, there were quite a few I thought around 1920. Oh, we had camp meeting every summer here in Viola. That's when I went with four towns, brother's name. We had a tent camp meeting up the creek here, two or three summers. And then when the faith people, what we call line-a-sides, then they had camp meeting over here. So, we had lots of, and we had traveling ministers. We had Sunday school and church up here at this church all my life until just recent. When they consolidated the schools, then that kind of took away the Sunday school and things from out here. People who were church people, they went to town to church. And they tried. Every summer we'd have Sunday school up here. And we had old preachers that belonged to the ministerial association. We'd have a preacher for a week or a month or so. So, we've had lots of...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What denomination were the camp meetings that you'd have every summer?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Well, that's what I said, the advance. We had an awful flock of Adventists. When they first begun to come up the creek, then they'd have camp meeting every summer. But these others, now there's quite a few faith people. You're not familiar with them. Just heard the name. Well, we don't know what they call themselves. We call them the faith people. But we always, when I was young, we called them line-a-sides because he was the first minister, old line-a-sides.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he around here?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh yeah, around here. And they preached. They... God, there were lots of people. And then they kind of died out a while and now there's a lot of them again.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was their preaching about?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: And what was their own special...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why faith?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Well...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: why?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: They're all... they're good people. They're kind of clammy, sir. Very good to each other. They're awful good to each other. But I think they think they're the only ones. Well, a lot of churches do that. They're the only ones that are saved. I would say because they... it don't matter what you do. It's all right. You ain't going where they are anyway. They kind of give you that impression. Although there's lots of nice... we've got friends that are in the faith people. And if we go to church with them, they'd stop and take us every time we go. But I didn't see the youths. But they're good people and they're good to each other if they're own members. They're sacred even.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you go to the Adventist meetings at all?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, yes. I was a kid. We went... yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they pretty emotional?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yes. The one... they used to have meetings around up at the schoolhouse, you know, us kids used to go. But I can remember... one old lady done quite a lot of preaching. She was one of the first ones here and they didn't have an established minister. And she always expected... I can remember that since I was a kid. She expected the end of the world was coming when they moved here, you know. My mother's sitting in the wheelchair and I said, Mrs. Shields passed away and my mother said, now she got out of the world just the same as the rest of us, we all. After she had preached, that was 60 years later, I'd imagine. That we were all going to be gathered up in the clouds was really the idea that the end of the world was just around the corner of the city. Yeah, they preached it all the time that the last days are here. And I don't know what they preach now because there's not many Adventists left. They've all passed away or moved away. There's only a family or two and they've got pretty worldly. They're not so narrow-minded as they used to be. Well, when they were against... Oh, they thought that they were the end of the world. It was going to be gathered up in the clouds. That woman thought it and preached it and...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Ever set a time?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, there's been lots of time set. My mother said one time back before they moved out here, they sold their things and set and waited for the end of the world to come. And that was an Advent settlement. So she was familiar with this end of the world here when this old lady preached it. Yes, she thought that, that the end of the world was coming. And there being other worldly instead of this worldly, now I know there aren't they...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is it meat that they're against eating or something?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, yeah, they don't eat much meat. The pigs, they don't eat pork. These don't eat much. These were mostly vegetarians that lived around here, although they did eat beef and chicken. But pork, you can't eat pork nor clams or what with a shell on them for some. I've read what that reason was, but I've forgotten.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what other ways were they other worldly instead of this worldly?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Did they work less on their farms just like they were... Good workers, but not on Saturday. If they wanted to borrow something, they'd come Sunday morning. That always made me mad. If they wanted to borrow something, they'd come Sunday morning. But if you left them entirely alone on Saturday, that was their worshipping day. But you were worshipping less. Well, but we worshipped Sunday. We kept Sunday, but we were keeping the wrong day. It didn't amount. That's one thing they was pretty snotty about. They didn't keep your day. I can remember one time we was picking peas when this Byron Shefflin's wife was picking peas. And the boss come and said, We've got a rush order for Sunday. If some of you want to work, you can work Saturday and Sunday. I don't remember whether we did or not, but I can remember Verna saying to a bunch of us that rode in the same car with her, said, Well, you're just as well at work. You're keeping the wrong day anyway. So we'll wait and see when she's still living up the creek and I'm living here. We'll see some day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who's kept the wrong day?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Or if we both kept the right one. Yeah, she said, You're just as well at work. You're keeping the wrong day anyway. Catholics are kind of like that, too. If you don't do what they believe, you know. When I was in training, we used to get orders to work on Sunday so that Catholic girls could go to Mass. And so they got to call us the heaters that had to work on Sunday.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that in Portland?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: In training school. They would do a Mass so they could get off, but we didn't have any excuse, so we had to work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, the church here, the local church, what denomination was there?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: It was just a community church. And the end of it, there was two men. One was a Nazarene and one went to the Christian church, and they got to quarreling, So. They each wanted to fix up the church and put their money in. And so they just kind of broke up altogether. I don't know how it did come out. That was Willis and Ed Gray. Eddie Gray. No, Art Ross used to say, I'm a Moscow Nazarene, Ed's a Plus Nazarene.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's the difference?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Well, shouldn't have been any. Shouldn't have been any, but he used to say it that way. Because Ed Gray kind of bossed the Nazarene, put his money in the Nazarene church in Plus. But he's finally moved close to Moscow, too. He fell out over there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when you were growing up and going to the community church, was it leaning towards one denomination?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Well, I think Christian church. This has always been a Christian church, I think. Of course, when my folks come, they come from Scotland. They're all Presbyterians, pure and simple.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I don't know what they, I can remember our dad bringing us to church every Sunday, do you, in Sunday school?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: But I never knew what they, just sort of religious people, that's all. But I'm sure this was always a Christian church. It's kind of like, I always say, Billy Graham's a good talker. And he always can't hardly wait to get to heaven. But when he gets sick, he gets the very best physician around. He ain't so anxious when it comes right down. He just like the rest of us. He's going to live as long as he can. In the Adventist revivals, were they, now I know some of the evangelists, like United Brethren, which I've heard quite a bit over in the South, you know, around Kendrick, very strong. They were quite emotional in their revivals.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think the Adventist was the same type as that?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Almost. Almost. That's what you said, in their ways. Got some good speakers, too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they get a lot of converts here when they started?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Well, when they come up our way, our home is three miles up the creek. And between here and Viola, there was a settlement. The Advent's bought all the land. And they had a school, Advent School up there on the hill at one time, boarding school even, and school. And so the converts, and some come from far away, but there were lots of families who were Adventists, moved in here. And they were supposed to have a, they were going to make breakfast food. But that never materialized. So they moved away one by one. That's why they moved away, because... No work here. Laura mentioned to me that you said that they wanted to buy one. Oh, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about your place? Did a lot of people sell their places too?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And moved away?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Uh-huh… so it really changed the conversation of the... Oh, yes. When the Advent's were here, there was an awful lot of Advent's around. And that was sort of a settlement, but as time went on, they went away. And other people come. Well, there's lots more people here than there was in them days, you know. I could walk to Viola, and it only passed three or four houses, and now there's a trailer house or a house every little bit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say it's a settlement, were they all clustered together?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Well, around in the hills, I'd say all... They didn't have 160 acres like... No. Some of the bigger that had money, buy, bought, and then they'd sell a little place and build a house.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know a part of the country where they were coming from?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: No, I really don't. I think down at College Place, maybe Walla Walla, a lot of them come from, you know, College Places and Advent Town. But there's only the Scheffern family about, so I can think of the Advent's now. I said we was here before them, and we outlived most of them. Did they mix very much with the... It seemed like at first they didn't much, but this time went on, I guess. They found out we wasn't any worse than they was as a rule. Well, it must have really changed the community if many people sold out and left when they came... Quite a few, it did. And the Advent school, you know, there was one old lady, Mrs. Baden, always come around before school starts. And if any of your children will go to school, I'll pay tuition. She would give that order, I can remember. She's come to my house.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She would pay their tuition so they could go?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: If they would go to the Advent school to make it a few more, when they begin to get a little scarce. She was an old lady, and she is a good Advent, and she owned land and had a little money, and she'd always say, If anybody wants to come to our school, I'll pay their tuition. And I don't know if she got very many victims or not, but I suppose she got a few.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know what the instruction was like at the school?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: No, they're just good, only they taught Bible at school. They were alright, and I guess had good... I think their credentials probably were alright, because there's some of them that have been pretty smart kids. All them Shefflin kids have, you know, Jim Shefflin, that's the head of the music down at WSU.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know him?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: He was raised here. Oh, I know him, yes, but... His dad went to school with my daughter at Viola later. You see, the school kind of faded away up there after they didn't have, well, like a lot of other private schools. I guess the money, too few people to make enough money to hire the teachers and things, because Howard went to Viola a year or two, but they also went up to Spangle a lot, and that's an Advent school up there. And I don't know how Howard, but I think it was because his dad wasn't very well, and he had to help with the chores and do that, I think that's why, because him and Shirley always were tight together, they always nip and tuck.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did many of the people leave, actually leave the community church to go to the Adventist church after they came?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: No, it seems though the Adventists, everybody knew about them before they come. They used to go to meetings, and to us kids did, and I suppose some of the older, but not many. But everybody knew they were coming. Well, knew what their religion was at the end of the world was here. I imagine a lot of people, most people couldn't accept that. They couldn't hardly accept that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But that's like the Nazarenes, and no, what is, what was that man used to always visit us? Was it Jehovah's?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yeah, we used to be pestered with them when we lived up the creek, traveling around every summer, this old fellow, they thought, they thought the end of the world was coming. I think there's quite a few.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that many years ago?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: No, that's not been 10, 15 years ago. And he said, well, you want to be prepared because it's going to be right away. And I said, the second coming to Christ, that's what they always preached, the Jehovah Witness. And I said, well, we've been looking for 2 ,000 years, and I don't think you or I, either one will ever see him. I don't know what he thought of that, but he didn't think I was very smart saying it, but I didn't care.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did the Adventists talk to you about two weeks ago?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, yes, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Persuade?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Right. When they first come here, but nobody listened to them, so they kind of quit. But they have a good school over at Moscow. They still have Advent school. You know toward Troy, don't you? You probably know where it is. Yes, yes. I kind of forgotten about it. And, as, and out about where you lived, there's quite a few Adventists. That's that Leroy Carlson and all his relatives.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that right? Well, were they here when you were a little kid?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: I wondered when they, about when they came. No, I was about probably 14 or 15 when they began to come in here. And I don't know if they'd been over at Troy before, or if that's just a branch of the ones that come here. I really don't know. But they seemed to, quite a lot of them had quite a little money, the Abadens and the Shefflins and the Shultz's. And they bought the land, and then as more come in, they'd sell off a little bit. My dad sold ten acres to them people, and they built a house, and then they let it go back finally. There was a few meals and things that they worked at when they first come here. And then as the meals...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That they owned?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: No, they didn't own, but they could find work and harvest in the summer. I mean harvest in the fall. But as time went on, then they found something better, I guess. So most of them were pretty poor. Well, just ordinary. A few of them had a little money, but not, not a lot.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But most of the people, like most of the families around here, didn't they own land and farm like this?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yes, own land, but I don't know, everything was so cheap, you just barely made a living. You had to have a cow and pigs and chickens in order to live, and that's about the way everybody done. Some were a little bigger farmers, a little more land.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your family, when you were working, or when your sisters worked, did the money that you made, was it your money, or did it go to help the family? I think it was all ours, wasn't it?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: I think so. We used to buy books and... I can remember the girls that go on after, if they went for the cookhouse that fall, I can remember them splurging to go to school, get new coats and clothes, and I can... I wasn't quite big enough to have any money then, so I can remember that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it important in your family to go on to school as much as you could?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yes, I think our father wanted to go on to school. Yeah, I think so. Our oldest sister got married when she was 17, and he always felt bad about that. Yeah. That was much school as she should have.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, are there any boys in your family?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Our only brother was killed right up there where we lived when he was 17.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it a logging accident?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: No, my dad and him fell a tree on him, a big limb just coming.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I was three months old, and Jenny can remember, can't you? A little?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yeah, that was a terrible time. The only boy they had... I guess they had a baby boy back east that didn't live, but that was the only... And my dad always needed a boy so bad with all the girls, but that's what happened. But he had been someplace to school the winter. I spoke and or tried school. Some friends took him and he went to school. Yeah, that was a bad...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you hear how it happened?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, they... No, we didn't know just how it happened. They were all around. Yeah, don't listen. They were up on the hill, the girls know. He was turning it all the way in a big limb.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did any of the girls help him in the field?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, I rode horseback all my life and herded cows.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you do farm work for him too?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: No, I don't think I ever. Well, we didn't have too much farm work. But the neighbors down, we didn't run the plow and do things like that. That some girls, some of the girls did, you know, work in the field and in the hay, but I don't think we ever did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that because you think they didn't want you to?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, I think my dad didn't believe in the girls, so he thought that was men's work, I believe. Mother helped him when he was in the field.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, did she help in the field?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, when they burned brush. She'd clean off of some land every year and they'd burn brush and I think that's about all she'd done. And then we got to having strawberries and everybody worked in the strawberries.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there a lot of tending to do with the strawberries?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, yes, I don't remember a whole one, but we picked strawberries. That was about a month. But I don't know, we helped set out the strawberry plants, but I don't remember a whole one much. Us girls didn't. They picked a lot. Yeah, we picked lots of strawberries.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I guess a silly thing, I was thinking, when you grew up, what kind of ambition did you have about what you wanted to do when you were a dad?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, I don't know, it's hard to tell. I always thought I was going to be a school teacher, but I never got there. Well, being a nurse isn't that as good as a school teacher. Maybe harder to work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: For you, what were you thinking of? I mean, what were the ideals that girls had in those days?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: I don't know, just about like they do now. Grow up, I guess, and get married, or I don't know, any different than any other. I guess they go to school and go to beauty shop a little better than they did then. I think we all thought we'd get to be school teachers, I believe. That's probably about what we thought we'd do. Well, the way that I have heard school teachers tell me, it seemed like they couldn't teach once they got married, though, in most part. Most of them kind of dropped quickly. I guess they did, too, get married and then quit. About a half, all my friends did, at least. Oh, the dote. She kept on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She kept teaching, didn't she? Or did you want to be a farmwife? Is that what you kind of expected?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Well, I really don't know. I really can't remember that. I suppose I wanted a little— You was always ambitious to have a little more money and a little better than you was. I guess you just expected to get that some way or other.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Didn't you want to go to Hollywood and get a movie?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: I don't think so. No, I don't think that was my signature.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, I don't think anybody— You didn't know enough about it, then?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: No. I don't think interested in it. No, I don't think so.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did you meet your husband?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, probably went with his mother at the cook house. Helped her at the cook house after I got acquainted with him. That's probably the start of that. Oh, his family just farmed around. Then you get acquainted around at parties or dances or something. That's about the way. Just how kids get acquainted now. Well, I don't know. It's quite the same. This only grandson that I've got, he grew up in this house here. When the war was over, a boy across the street brought a woman and a little girl home with him. He'd found her someplace. That was his stepdaughter. Well, Charles and her went to school all their life together on the bus here in Viola. He'd never seen her at all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But after he'd come home from the war, why?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: He couldn't see anybody but Marilyn.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wasn't that funny?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: They'd lived across the street for eight, ten years. Just that happened. Just couldn't see anybody but Marilyn. She had grown up and he'd grown a little older. But it was kind of funny. I used to babysit for him and her when Shirley and Vernon and Sonny and Cleo would go someplace. Well, they never paid and he'd be sitting studying and she'd be just a little girl, you know. It was funny. We never thought about them, ever. They've got a nice home and live up the creek and he's plant superintendent and potlatch.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How old were you when you got married?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Nineteen. Nineteen.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that about the age that a lot of people were getting married then?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: I think kind of. I don't know. I think they're supposed to be married older now, but I can just see a lot of them. Just eighteen, nineteen, twenty.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that makes any difference?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Whether you wait long or not?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: No, I don't think it does. I think, I don't know, they've all had so many divorces and things now. It's just a trend. They'll get over that someday too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were growing up, what was the attitude about divorces?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: They didn't have divorces so many. When you got married, you got a bad one, you stayed and worked it out. But then, later then, now, like now, I think it's about as bad as it'll get. It'll get the other way, go the other way. Everybody's got it almost that you see. When we lived up home here in the last ten years, I babysat around for, oh, I didn't have anything to do, I could go. Well, pretty near every place I babysat, well, they either had a stepdad or a stepmother. And I said to them hall kids, I said, everybody you know has got a stepmother or a stepdad. They just know whole families.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that ten years ago?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yeah, but right now... It got a lot worse since then. Well, it has got worse. That was the beginning. Yes, just everybody now, pretty near. The younger ones. Looking back at it then, the idea that you had to stay together, you think it was a good idea or do you think it restricted... I don't know whether it was any worse, but it's now. I can see why they do now. The girls are just as independent as the man and get to work, and if you've got a little money, you don't have to stay with him. You can just get out and go, and that's why it's happening so bad now, because the girls are working. They get just as big money as the man they don't have to be. I think they'll get over that, I believe. I don't know, but I think maybe they will.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did it seem to you that in those days, a lot of marriages were kind of unhappy?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, I don't think so. They all worked hard and raised their kids, and I guess that's... But you had to stay together and you didn't want to stay. Well... Or did you want to stay together because you had to. Oh, I don't think everybody wanted to divorce like they do. That's the first thing they think about nowadays. If they quarrel or something, why are they just leave, that's all. They didn't seem to do that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you see the male come?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Leave, they'll be looking for me, probably. I think the dogs would bark if...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I don't like these short days, do you?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: How close people would watch their money in those days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think it was very close?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, indeed. My sister that and then spoke and said, the dollars come so easy nowadays and said, I just feel bad, my dad. The dollars were so hard to make. Oh, yes. But there was other things. They worked and raised garden and things. They didn't depend so much on the dollars. You couldn't live without money now, and they lived, maybe with very little money. Had plenty to eat. All you bought was coffee and sugar, and some people didn't use that. The flour was cheap and raised most everything you ate. Never thought about needing money every minute. Oh, that was different. I know that. A little money went a long ways them days. Well, you bought a lot more with your little bit of money that you had. Was another thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know many families where the man had a drinking problem in those days?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yeah, I know a few. Because I've had the idea that that was quite a problem back then. Well, I don't think it was quite as bad as it is now. I don't believe it was quite as bad as it is now. But other than more people, I don't know. Maybe it was just the same.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it worse now?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: I don't either. I don't know whether you'd say it's worse now. But it would be the man that would have a drinking problem without his wife. Yeah, I don't know in my lifetime, I don't know any women. I don't think that had a drinking problem. And they say there's so many now. We don't know about them, but I know one or two that do have. But you'd never know it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what would she do when her husband started drinking up the family earnings?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, well, lots of times they went out and worked or something like that. Separated, probably. But I don't know whether it had been worse then or now. They say there's awful lots of drinking now, but of course I'm not around to know about it. Don't know about it… so...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I was going to ask you too, when you got married, did you find that you could make do on very little? Did you have much when you got married?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, I don't know. I guess we had a team and a cow and a few chickens, I guess. They started out about like everybody else then. Around here, I guess in town, they had done a little differently. I don't know. But about everybody I knew was about in the same boat, I'd say.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have your own farm at the time?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: No, we rented the farm at first and then we... He rented my dad's place in later years and that's the way it was. And we had a little place of our own later too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When the Depression hit here, was the 30s a lot worse than the 20s?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: No, we didn't know. I can't remember the Depression at all. It seemed like we had just as much. As much as we did any other time the way we lived. People who worked and lost their job, that was different. But we lived on a ranch and raised things. I didn't think it was any different than any other time. I can remember when they say, in 93, that's the year I was born. That was the bad Depression when a lot of people lost their land. And the land banks got everything. Well, my dad said that was about the best year he had because there was so much spoiled grain that they'd just bring it for a little wood and he could feed his cattle. And he said that was about as good a year as he had. So it worked both ways.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he lose his grain that year?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Well, he didn't have much. He only made wood and had cattle to feed. And the farmers needed wood because they had wood burning stoves. And he said he could get lots of grain that was spoiled a little, but it was good enough for cattle feed. And they had to have wood and they just traded for everything. Wood. So it wasn't any worse. I think that's the year he said he built the house or got the lumber to build it. Wow. That's pretty good. But a lot of people went broke on their farms, had to begin all over again. Some people have said to me that right after World War I in their 1920, 2022, they were real rough years on the farm because the prices were low.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember it as being a hard time?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: I think probably the big farmers, but now they complain they don't get enough. And I can remember the last oats that we sold the last few years. If we got $35 a ton for oats, and now it's $108, I see the price on television. And still they complain. But we farmed with horses. Didn't take so much to farm them days. So I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you making enough money? Well, were you making enough to save in those days?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: No. We never saved any. You done good to buy what you needed, I think. Oh, we bought a car. A tin lid when the new cars come out. I guess by hook and crook it's just like it is now. They just, some people had a little money and some people spent it all, and some people wasn't dead. I don't see much difference. It seems like there's a lot more money kicking around.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Ain't there?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: What I think about that. Why there is. They talk about the good old days, and I can see good things then. We didn't have so much money, but we had other things. Like, we say other things. Well, we were more so. Country people sure don't. And I don't know what they do in town. I don't think they do in town because they don't know their neighbors. I can see that to people that I know, don't even know their, maybe I'll know them from out here, from coming to club or meeting them someplace, they don't know who their next door neighbors are. I know it's more sociable those days, about that way. But it's for having money. Why there's more money these kids get more money than the men did used to, and are not happy unless they do get it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did you start after you were married? When was the first working out that you did, every time you brought money into that family?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, I never helped. Maybe help a woman in the house in harvest or something. I never worked for a long time then until my kids were bigger.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you find it was a full time work just to keep the home?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, yeah, sure. Well, my mother was old at that time, and we lived next door. And so then as the time went on, she got in a wheelchair. So I was pretty busy for quite a few years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You were caring for her?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Well, helping. My dad was, uh...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: About eight years? Huh? About eight years?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: She was eight years in a wheelchair. So I've been busy all my life. Doing something sometimes didn't amount to much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did you live in the same house as your dad?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: No, no.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you took care of her house and yours?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yes, and I wasn't very good housekeeper to boot, so...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You weren't?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: So I said it's kind of hard to keep two houses when you ain't a very good housekeeper in the first place, but... Oh, yes. We washed for her and we done lots. Well, this may be a dumb question. I want to keep making talk about work, but I really am curious.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there a lot of work that you had to do in a house then that you don't have to do anymore?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: No, everything. Well, we didn't have things as handy as I have. We had to do it for seven later years, but then you always had to cook and wash and keep your kids clean and send them to school. I don't see a bit of difference what the women do now as what they did then. Only most of them have automatic washers and a few things, but there's probably other things they do that we didn't have to do then. I don't know. Well, it just seems to me like they got a lot of free time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yeah, through the way it was then. Oh, indeed. It's not just put up and all ready to almost put on the table. But it's just like one sister said, you can stay busy if you want to doing nothing. And some people can get a lot done in that same time, but they think they're just as busy. And it seems like it works out that way. I don't know why. Well, you might have was in the wheelchair.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was wrong?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, it just got old and thought she said I had one hip that never was any good and it just give out. I don't think she had a stroke or anything and just sort of enjoyed being waiting on. I said if she had had the ambition that my dad had, she wouldn't have stayed in the wheelchair, but she did. And he lived to be like a month of being ninety nine years old and only about three months that he was just a little bit over the hill. He was pretty smart. I always said when he was eighty five and we were putting up hay or I was helping, I said him and I made as good a man as the other men who were working. You and him and I together. But he was still working at eighty five. Oh, yes. He was a pretty good man at eighty five. Had bees. Him and I used to extract probably four hundred pounds of honey a year. That was when they couldn't get sugar. They just beat a path to our door. We didn't have a bit of trouble selling our honey.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When was it they couldn't get it? Don't you remember when sugar was rationed? World War II or World War I?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Must have been the tape. I think it was during the war, but I think it marked the honey in his boat. Well, now I can't just remember, but all of a sudden, then, come on, why it wasn't so much sale for honey.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But just the other day I paid a dollar and a half for a little glass of honey probably wouldn't be a pint, would it?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Probably supposed to be a pint. I said we sold buckets and buckets for a dollar and a quarter, half gallon. Ten buckets we put it up in. But that's the difference. There wasn't no money then, but things didn't cost so much. If you had a little money, you'd get about as much as you would now with all the money that's in circulation. I can't hardly believe it, but it seems to be the way of the world. Well, does that mean that you're saying that when the Depression came, what they called the Depression from about 1931... That was before that. That had to be before that. It was before World War I. Must have been the tail end of World War I. I think there was rationing. But when that Depression came, you really didn't feel it. I don't think we did. I don't believe we did. Things were about the same as they did then. But then the prices for crops were down, I know. But we were getting more because I can remember my dad when we'd sell a veal and got so much money. I don't know. I suppose we used to sell them for $15. Come in. I brought your paper. Thank you very much.

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Then when we sell a veal and get $35 or $40, he thought that was wonderful. But he didn't realize on the other tail end we was paying quite a little more for groceries that we bought. But that was wonderful to get so much more for a veal. So, I don't know. It worked both ways.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, as a renting plan, were you interested in buying?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, well, we had a little place next to our dads and he just farmed them all. That was after my dad got old, too old to... And we milked cows and we done lots of things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that uncommon for somebody who paid $85 and liked your father?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, well, he sort of outlived a lot of the neighbors, but then we had lots of old neighbors, too. We had lots of old neighbors. That was the first. A neighbor down the way, that's the first cancer I remembered.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And the men used to go set up with old man names, you remember?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: But now, just every other person has got a cancer. Women and men both. I know you talk about the way couples used to get along. You mentioned that one of the Comerford boys shot and killed himself. That was over his wife.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the story? Do you remember? Remember, Jackson? How did I happen to tell that?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Well, you were mentioning to Laura. I just listened to it. And I had heard something about it before. Well, that was in the Potlatch store. And he worked in the men's department. And he married a brown girl.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Jack Comerford. And he shot himself in the shoe store. And I guess that was over his wife, but I can't remember. Yes, she fell in love with his brother.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wasn't that the one?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Married his brother after that, I do believe. I believe that's the way of it. It's been so long ago, but I can remember. When they went to work in the morning, there he had struggled around. There was blood all over that Potlatch shoe store. I can remember him telling that. Yeah, and that was Jack Comerford. I don't think they didn't have any kids, I'm sure. But I believe it was brother or brother-in-law that she...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever hear that doll they called Psychiana Robinson in law school?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, indeed. I put envelopes full of papers there for three years. You worked for him. Never saw him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He was quite a man, wasn't it?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Doc Robinson. A rather noted figure, I must say. Indeed. Where... Oh, you've just heard from Dick... I heard all over him. Well, that was funny. We used to get somebody moved away from here and wrote back and said, did we ever hear of Doc... of papers? Other places, you know, had big write-ups about him, but nothing ever here in the Moscow paper… yeah, his fame...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: ... Did you ever heard of him?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: His fame had spread far away, but not... Of course, everybody right in Moscow knew who he was and all that, but didn't pay any attention to him. Like they did from... at other places.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know what the women that worked there pretty much thought of him?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, it was just a good job, and the mail that was putting up mail kept, I don't know, probably ten of us. Stuff and papers. They must have bought...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he a real doctor?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, yeah, I don't know. He was sort of... He was a religious leader. Yeah. He was a religion person. And he had a lot of pull. People... I can remember the bosses where we worked would say, some people come in there and say they wanted to see him so bad. Of course, he was never around where we were. But they said if they could just touch... just look at him or touch him, they could be cured. He had that much power over some people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it hard for them to see him?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: I don't think he ever was around much in Moscow. I don't know if you could see him or not. I really don't. No. So that was another job that people around here were controlling. Yeah. Well, a lot of people worked there different... Oh, yes, he had big crews sometimes. It wasn't very much of a crew when I worked. He was kind of fading out. But one summer, or maybe two summers, I worked there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was when you were off the pea picking?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yeah, that was later. But I think that was going on quite a lot. It got pretty much during pea picking time too, because some of the gals who had picked peas worked there. It didn't go on all the same time, but I think kind of the stuff in the envelopes come along just as the pea picking was sort of slacking off. I believe that was the way of it. I want to ask you just a little bit about the socializing in those days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it mostly like just two families getting together, or was it more neighbors?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Old neighbors. We used to have parties and dances, and yes, lots more than nowadays.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where would they be?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, this dance hall up here, I don't suppose they could hit a dance. In them days, we all went.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was a dance hall?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yeah, right up here on the hill.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was outside of town?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Right here in the city of Iola, but everybody come from miles around, Christmas and holidays, and maybe once a month they'd have a dance up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it private owned or community?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Somebody owned the... But somebody would run the dance, have an orchestra, hire the hall for all winter or something. Yes, they don't have any dances like that around in the country. I think they do at the pool halls maybe when they drink and dance. I don't know anything about that. And at the moose and elks, they still dance, but we don't have any country dances like we used to. There's none around any place. I don't know just why. Kids nowadays don't dance. I mean, high school kids dance, but I guess other people do, but no country dances… but...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there much vision back and forth among the families?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, yes. Everybody knew everybody else and visited. A lot more than they do now. They'd scare you if you'd seen a family coming to get dinner for them Sunday, and always expected to do that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I think, wouldn't it?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Scare everybody if they'd seen a family come to get Sunday dinner. You always went home with somebody or head company after church and Sunday school. But that's all socializing, and all the weeks they work. Now the kids go... School kids, all of them got a car. They go to town every night. Do something. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: These dances, were the people all ages come to the community hall?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yeah, big and little. They didn't have babysitters. Sometimes there'd be a whole lot of babies or little kids sitting around. Yeah, whole families come.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would there be any food to eat there?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, yes. Always dinner at midnight. Sandwiches and things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Coffee?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yeah. People just bring them in. Oh, no. Usually the man that was running the dance, you had to buy your supper. He'd have somebody preparing it. Yeah, that's really gone.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And when they danced, did you dance with a lot of different people?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Sure. They finally got then. And later years you danced with just the two couples that you come with, or maybe your own.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But why would dance with everybody?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Everybody knew everybody else. Yep. That sure got funny after a while. That they're getting back to ballroom dancing again now. You see it on television. But the kids, they don't know nothing about dance, and they hop around like... It's true. That's right. Now, you take the club like the Quilting Club that you have now. In those days, were there any clubs such as... Well, now in World War I, they met around at the houses here, and they knit, and they made stockings for the man, and then went on. Pretty soon then they had... Was that Lady's Aid, or was that a local... That was... They always called it Club here. I don't think church... Old church women come too. In town, it was always Lady's Aids. But out here, they knit and did things for that, and then it just went on and on. This is about the oldest club in the county, I think.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It's the same club?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, yeah. Just went on. Half of them are... I mean, the old ones die off, and new ones come on. Same club. It used to be an extension club from the university or the county, you know. But we don't have her come out anymore, because nobody... They're getting a few new young ones now, but it got to be the mostly old ones, and we'd had all that dressmaking and cooking and what have you, and we didn't want that anymore. So we just didn't pay in our dues anymore, so they dropped it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was an extension club? Was that after the first war that it became an extension club?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Mm-hmm.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know if we're trying to, say, reconstruct the history of the club, was the beginning of it first World War, or did it start before then, even?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: I would imagine even before first World War, and then it got a little better during the war, and then it just always went on and on, and met at the houses first, and then up in the old school house when they didn't have school there after the county was consolidated. And then it went on and on until they built a little house up there, and now we're going to wear it out, I guess, this bunch, or the next bunch will probably. Yeah, always been something doing there every Thursday for many, many years. So you think it really never... It never stopped after... No, it never stopped. ...first World War. But at one time, it was like they still have extension clubs around, where this Joanne Anderson, she was out here the other day demonstrating the Crock Pots, cooking for the 4-H girls, but the 4-H girls had to go to school, so the other old ladies had to come. She had about a dozen old ladies. I didn't go, but a lot of them did. And she would come again, you know, she's always looking for something to do, which is all right. She's doing her job, but we don't need her.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it useful in there today, that expedient decision?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: I don't think that it ever was, but that's one man's opinion. I think you talked to other women, you would think it was all right. She gets good wages, and she puts in her time, but I can tell you here just how it works out to me now. Maybe these young ones who want to learn things, but you know, they learn so much in the books, they don't need her. I think that's surplus money that's spent, but that's just my opinion. But we went to a dinner at the moose, you know, the moose now is giving free dinners to old people. Well, I didn't go free, I pay for my dinner, and I've only been twice. But one day that I was there, she talked, and she was paid to do the speech after dinner, and I bet she spent an hour and a half telling all old people like me, half of them was asleep before she got done, and I just couldn't hardly sit there. I wanted to get up and go, telling us how to buy and what to buy. Well, we've done that before she was born, till it's just boring. Now, if you know what it was... I can believe you. I can really believe you. Well, that's just exactly what it was. Wasn't nobody interested at all with her speech, but she was doing what she was supposed to do, and doing a good job, telling us what kind of cheese to buy, a little bit cheaper than the other. Well, heck us old ladies, if we want some cheese, we know enough kind of cheese to buy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what about back when they had it in its club 40 years ago, when they had it here? What was the purpose of it then?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Well, she come, and they used to have dressmaking, and they had always taught few ceramics up there, and just different things that the club tries to keep at the university. There's been lots of different women that have been Extension agents.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think it was good for the people back there?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Maybe, I don't think it was. I think we knew just perhaps.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you have any skills for you?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: I think we knew somebody in our group who was good seamstresses, better probably than she ever was. She was just doing her job all them years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did you first yourself have anything to do with that club?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, I used to go once in a while when my kids were little. I had 60 years ago when they met around the houses.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then? World War I?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Probably before World War I. And then I just went, I'll tell you, I haven't been a steady member for long. When I picked peas, I used to go to the dinners and stop once in a while when I had a day that happened to me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But here, since Jenny moved up here, we went pretty regular in the last 20 years, ain't we?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yeah, I have. 20. Al and I went when I wasn't working.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what did the club do besides making the clothes during the war? What did they do?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Well, they sold. They always had some kind of project. We've got a glass cutter up there, and we had mostly it's sociable. We quilt. But they used to sew. They got a sewing machine. They must have sewed. Well, they done dressmaking a while.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long was that quilt? Over chairs a while?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Over chairs. Well, a post-string. We've had a class in all them different things that they do now for these younger ones at the university. But we've out-grilled it. Well, if they learn to do it, they don't need to be taught always. They can do it. But...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You used to teach it at the club?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: That extension woman would come out when she had a class. And so everybody knows how to do it, or anybody's interested.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long has the quilting been going on there? Ever since you can remember?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: They've been quilting for...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They quilted when you first started, didn't they?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: In 50. And more or less all this time. They've done other few things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You didn't do it back in the 30s too, or before?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: I don't know. When they did start. I know you can't pin it down to that. I guess ever since the club started, they've all... No. I don't think they... Well, they did make quilts too, but they didn't quilt at the clubhouse, I don't believe. I don't know how long that is. Probably 30 years though.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, was that the only club around?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: You mentioned one other when we were starting to talk. I can't remember the name of it. It wasn't Rebecca, but it was that. Oh, Royal Neighbors in Town. Oh, that's just Lodge. That's just Lodge. Royal Neighbors. And the Royal Neighbors in Grange and Eastern Star, all them in town. In town, you mean in... Wow. No, Moscow. Moscow. No, we don't have any Grange out here, but a lot of people belong to the Grange from out here. So... But... But the quilting seemed to settle down and everybody needed a quilt, so that's been going pretty strong for... I guess, Pritner, ever since we built this. That woman wanted to know the other day how long this might have to ask somebody else. I don't know how long we've had that clubhouse. I remember... That was before... That was before... Well, sometime early 40s, I guess. One woman, they kind of tried to collect a little money. We used to give dinners and things for the benefit of the club, you know. One woman wouldn't donate any money. She said the clubhouse, shouldn't have put it there, was going to slide off into the creek. Well, she slid into the next world years ago and the clubhouse still set.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you always call it just the club?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Community, Viola Community Club. We got a... We can write checks with the Viola Community Club and they accept them. You know, it seems awful nice when I think of the other communities around the county that you've had something that's had such a long... Well, you'd be surprised how many women come out. Somebody will invite somebody from the university while they just rave about it. They think this is the most sociable place they ever and the nicest people. Viola didn't always have such a good name, but... It's living the bad name down. Some of these old people will always have to bring in how many saloons Viola used to have, but they've all died. They passed into the next world too, all these people who used to... Now, I've heard that. I've heard people say that Viola in the early days, they were a tough place.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Isn't there any truth to Viola being a tough place?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Well, I don't know if it's any tough. They drank and I suppose rode horses and done a few things that people used to do in the early days. I don't think it's any worse than any other little town. I think it was all the same. I don't think it ever was any worse. It's funny that I've heard that too. Sure you do, anybody. Yeah, that seems like when there was a pool over here, well that's all you heard about it. It got pretty bad here some years when certain people had the tavern up here. It wasn't Viola people. It was people from other places, but they'd come and they'd fight and give Viola kind of a bad name. But then that ain't what you heard. I think what you heard years ago. Yeah, early days. Early days. But people around Viola were mean, tough… yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I don't think we ever hung only one man, did we? One man from Viola, I don't think he was raised here, was he?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Horse thief.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Don't you remember?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Somebody got hung down, walla walla, but he hadn't lived at Viola. But he wasn't hung.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wait a minute, was that the guy that was hung in a coat? Wasn't he in a coat bag? Are you talking about Hill? Who's told you that tale?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, it's in the history. Yeah, well that's who I'm thinking about. Yeah, I've been told this story too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wasn't it at Hill?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: I think, I wouldn't know, but I believe so. I believe so. But you know, that would have been Bud's brother or uncle. Anyway, the Una Hill has been the head of this club for ages. I wouldn't want her to even, she knows it of course, but I wouldn't even want to breathe it to any of the new people. But I think it would have been made.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he supposed to really, was he really guilty or? The Hill?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yeah. I think a horse straight. I think of stealing horses, I guess he probably was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did extreme?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Mm-hmm. Hangs somewhat for doing that. Yeah. It's a real vigilante. But I guess they did that years ago when they get to, probably hung some innocent ones too. Yes, we know all that Hill, both families, and it's funny now, Una's son farms on this side of the mountain, and the other hill boy from the other family farms, and they broke land clear up. We always laugh. The hills have met up on top of that mountain. They broke out. But they're good friends. These grand, they'd be second, third generation, you know, but they're farming the land at the other hills. You know, one other thing I wanted to ask you about was school consolidation, and what effect that had here. I heard it wasn't too good for the town. Well, it wasn't good for the community. It sure did break up the community. If you didn't, of course, now it wouldn't make any difference, although I'd hear news from my grandkids. But that sure broke up the community. We didn't have no card parties nor PTA meetings or anything where people got together. The kids went off to school and among strangers, and it sure did break up the community. And you know, they preached to us that that would be cheaper to have buses than to keep up so many schools, but we found out it wasn't. It got more expensive and more expensive. I think taxes around this town are half school taxes. Most anybody will tell you that, and maybe more than half right now. Of course, we've got so old we don't know too much about taxes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did people here oppose it when it came out?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: At first they did, but then we had good speakers come out, and it kind of got so it carried, so they got them consolidated, and everybody went to town. But I think I've heard their sense, oh, maybe they think they get better school, and I don't know. But we had smart enough kids when we had country schools. Do you think that the community, it sounds like you're saying that the community revolved around the school. Oh, well, it did, sort of. The school, and we always had church up here. Well, then when the kids all went to school, they got to go to Sunday school, and they got to go into Sunday school in town, and that's the way. We didn't have any school or church, so there was nothing to keep the community together. And that's just the way we've ended up now. We don't have.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But the club, did you got the club?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: We got the club, and that's a good thing, because a lot of these women wouldn't see anybody from year to year. They'd just go to town and get their groceries, and maybe certain people they would visit. But there'll be no community to hear the town gossip or news. Not all gossip. This ain't a very bad club to gossip. It's like that woman says, we don't repeat gossip twice if you want to listen first to close. Close the first time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You hear that on television?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: She always says that. A bunch of women sitting around, telling they don't gossip, so you better. We don't repeat gossip, so you better listen the first time it's around. And that's the way it is up here. People don't talk bad about each other. I don't think. I don't know. I don't believe they do.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In the early days, was there much gossip you think among them?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, I don't think so. They never had time to gossip.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Never seen each other often enough, did they?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: No, I don't think it's as bad as it is in town or some other places. I don't believe it is.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Does the club have the same regulars most of the most?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Next school district, or they bought little pieces of land. There's a nice mobile home, and some have got a little piece of land that have bought houses. Well, a lot of them women are starting to come because they feel like they're in the community, and I guess we've invited them. There's quite a few. A lot of several professors' wives that live up there. One by one they come, and then maybe they won't come anymore, and another one will get started. But there's a few locals that just sort of regular.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: About what? Maybe a dozen of them that are regular?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: About a dozen, regular. I'd say about that many. But it's kind of good to have a few new ones. Some of us old ones will drop off someday, and then there'll be somebody to... I guess it'll just go on and on until the clubhouse falls in. I don't know. I would certainly hope so. Yes, it's kind of nice to have it. And anybody's welcome.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you visit much outside of the club with people in the club?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Not a lot. We don't have any way to get out and visit, you know, anymore. I have to come visit you. Yeah, they come and visit us. But I don't think we would anyway. We didn't when we had to run in the car. We went to town once in a while. When we was out Sunday for dinner, she come and got us. That's our old neighbor up the creek. But I don't want people...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You still not running around working?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: No, I don't work no more. I wouldn't be surprised if you did. You seem like you could. Well, this woman down here, I packed her paper. She fell and broke her ankle or sprained it. I've took her paper to her here for a month or two. But she ain't as old as I am. But she just takes her pills and wonders what new elements she's going to develop.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever do working out in people's houses?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yes, I took care of a lot of people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this just later years?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yes, later years. Not too long ago I have. Jenny's been up here. She come up here in 47 from Walla Walla. And I've worked several places since in that time when she was up home. She'd hold down the fort and I worked. Oh yeah, I could work yet if I would. I mean, I've got chances I could take care of people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Probably younger than myself, but do they need something done?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: You know, it really seems to me like the feeling people have about work has changed a lot because... Gosh, I didn't have to get going for a long time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know what I mean?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: I don't know if you've seen it with young people as much as I have.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: People used to think it was kind of a disgrace to work, is that what you mean?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
