words: And now they're tickled to death to have a job. To get so much money. Yeah. I don't know where that idea would come from. It seems like for women mostly, they didn't want women to work. I know what, but they got liberated. They don't want to sit at home and they want to get out. That's what's the trouble now, these young ones at work. They want a little less big money. They want the money and they get too independent. That's what causes a lot of the divorces. I can see that among the kids around. But at the same time, it seems to me like people maybe like work less than they used to. It seems like people don't like their work. No, there's some darn poor workers, but they like the money.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that in your day when you were working that you and other people liked?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: I enjoyed your work more. Oh, some people do now. Some people enjoy working. But some don't, but I suspect it was always that way probably. Probably always that way. I'm thinking of PTA.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the working?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: IWW, it was a long time ago. I wasn't very big for that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When were the strikers?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yes. They went along the railroad tracks and bum food. That's about all I can remember. I'm thinking of the PTA.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No? What is it? Did the PTA start here in the third?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: No. That's teachers. No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think of the work, the depression work?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: The PTA and the... CC boys. I can remember all that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You came around here to CC boys?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yes. I was married and had a family, but this gal of mine, she used to go to dances around and get acquainted with the boys come out at that time. But the others then... You thought very much of it. Oh, some of them was. Edra married one. That's how he got out here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you married one?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, a Ross Fort girl. He first come out here. Chuck...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they have those daughters?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: I think so.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did people around here get on a public works program?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yes. A lot of them did. They had jobs. I didn't know anybody really that did. I think maybe we had a little road work. Maybe my husband hauled gravel on the road. I believe that was...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you say? WPA?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yes. Yeah. I can't think just what it means, but that's what it was. Works Progress Administration. Well, anyway, I remember that, but I just remember the initials of the IW. We always said, I won't work, but that wasn't. Were they thought... No. I don't think they were not thought to...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about surplus foods? Did you get any of those here?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Some people did surplus foods. Not much, but I believe some, a few families did. I heard it like that. Oh, yeah. We had the co-op meal.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wasn't that surplus? Co-op what?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: The meal up in the woods. Somebody run it, and they... Yeah. John worked at that. And you got lumber. You had to take it out in script. You could get lumber at the meal, or that's how you got paid. You didn't get cash, but I don't think we had that too long around here, but I remember that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that a nonprofit thing just for everybody?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: I think so. And they had gardens and canned stuff and sold it. I don't know how that got started. I don't seem to know too much about it. But that was during my time, and we headed around here, and it was a co-op meal. And I remember they made... Yes, they did. At Lula's house.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: We canned fruit, didn't we?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that depression or before?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: I don't know any more depression than I ever had. That doesn't... I heard like around Bovill, some people were almost starving. Well, I think they did, where they depended on a certain thing for jobs, you know, and it shut down. There was just nothing else to live on, but it didn't make much difference to us. When you lived on a farm, you just milked another cow and sold a little cream and things like that. That's why we didn't know about, but I know town people did. I've heard people talk about it. Well, you know how it would be living in town now. If you didn't have money to buy groceries, you'd go out. You wouldn't eat because there's no free stuff around. I had a place like Bovill. There was no... Well, I suppose they all depended on the meal or woods or whatever it was. He told me, he said, you want to get your parents long-lived, long-livered, and then so you could tell who your parents were going to be. He said that's the secret of your long life.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were your parents both long-lived?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Well, our mother died at 92. Oh, my father at 99. 99, so I guess you'd call it. Pretty long-livered scotch, I guess.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And what was your date of birth, Pam?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: I think Laura got... They all were really good people here and there. I don't know what they preach anymore than we're about to the end of the world.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Don't they think that?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yes, they think they're going to be getting a different size too. I kind of believe they do. So they say they're Bible students. They say they're Bible. But they're against wearing slacks. They don't wear slacks. They're much jewelry, and you get your hair done up in a little wad on top of your head, that's good. You can tell them. A mile away.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Does Marilyn have her hair on the back?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Yes, indeed she does. You don't wear slacks. She used to wear pretty slacksuits, but she doesn't anymore. Nobody says the reason. I don't know. I guess when you join the church you get into that. You learn a little more of their methods of doing that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember these lines, fella?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Oh, I can't read them. They always said he converted all the widows. I can just remember. This is gossip. They said it wasn't the religion. It was the minister that converted so many.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A lot of these old widows, John, you remember when he was here?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Two by two… that's what Mrs. Breilthold is called. She didn't call them Linus Knights. She called him Two by Two.
